g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture explores the critical relationship between cache and main memory in microcontroller systems.
The processor operates at gigahertz speeds while memory operates at megahertz, creating a critical speed gap.
g.text: Cache bridges this gap by storing frequently accessed data

g.data-narration: The memory hierarchy shows how fast, small cache sits between the CPU and slow, large main memory.
Cache works on two principles: temporal locality (recently used items will be used again) and spatial locality (nearby items will be accessed).
g.text: Recently accessed items will be accessed again
Items near recently accessed items will be accessed

g.data-narration: There are three main cache mapping techniques: direct mapping, fully associative, and set-associative. Each has trade-offs between hardware cost and hit rate.
In direct mapping, each main memory block maps to exactly one cache line using modulo arithmetic. Simple but may cause conflict misses.
g.text: | Tag (6 bits) | Index (8 bits) | Offset (6 bits) |

g.data-narration: Fully associative mapping allows any block in any cache line. Maximum flexibility yields lowest miss rate but requires expensive content-addressable memory.
Set-associative mapping divides cache into sets, each with multiple lines. A block can go in any line within a set, balancing cost and performance.
g.text: | Tag (12 bits) | Set Index (8 bits) | Offset (6 bits) |

g.data-narration: Write-through writes to both cache and main memory simultaneously, ensuring consistency but slower performance due to every write going to memory.
Write-back writes only to cache initially, updating main memory only when the block is replaced. Faster but requires dirty bit tracking.
The MESI protocol maintains cache coherence in multi-core systems with Modified, Exclusive, Shared, and Invalid states for each cache line.
Average Memory Access Time formula combines hit time with miss penalty. With 95% hit rate and 2ns hit time, AMAT is just 3 nanoseconds.
g.text: AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)
Given: Hit Time = 2ns, Miss Penalty = 20ns, Hit Rate = 95%

g.data-narration: For a 16KB direct-mapped cache with 64-byte blocks and 1MB main memory: 256 cache lines, needing 8 index bits and 6 offset bits.
A 4-way set-associative cache with 1024 sets and 64-byte blocks yields 256KB total cache size with 4096 total cache lines.
Key takeaways: cache exploits locality, mapping techniques trade hardware cost for hit rate, and write policies balance speed with consistency.
g.text: ✓ Cache exploits temporal and spatial locality
✓ Direct mapping: simple but conflict misses
✓ AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)

g.data-narration: In embedded systems, cache design directly impacts performance, power consumption, and real-time constraints. Choose wisely for your microcontroller application!
g.text: 🎯 Apply These Concepts to Optimize Your Embedded Systems!
